title: The $8.6B Self-Driving AI Backed by Nvidia and Uber | Alex Kendall, Wayve
transcript: autonomous driving is all about looking at the AV problem with an AI approach. There is an opportunity there to drive accidents down to near zero. So, what we did is we raised 1 and 1/2 million dollars, got some friends together, we rented a house, and we put our car in the garage and started hacking away. Taking it from expensive retrofit vehicles which relied on compute, HD maps, infrastructure to mass market vehicles that you can buy or manufacture for 30, 40, 50,000 dollars each that have inbuilt hardware that's in global supply chains, doesn't need HD maps, or can drive anywhere. We're now the first company to have driven zero shot in over 500 cities throughout Europe, Asia, North America. It's also driven in over 10 different cars from electric vehicles to vans to SUVs. We went north of the Arctic Circle to test the driving in 22-hour darkness and snow. We're even in Tokyo during a typhoon where local trains and buses shut down. No disengagements, perfect autonomy the whole day. And it demonstrated that we truly can generalize to any vehicle anywhere. I think now the question is how do you bring it to global scale? And that's the shift that we want to take the industry through where every vehicle is capable of driverless operation, which is clearly the steady state of where we're going to go to. You're listening to Gradient Descent, a show about making machine learning work in the real world, and I'm your host, Lucas Bewald. All right. Here we are at the Core Weave House at NVIDIA GTC. I just got done interviewing Alex Kendall, who's the CEO of Wave, a company that I've admired for a long time and has done phenomenally well in self-driving. And I think you can actually see him behind me messing with the Core Weave F1 car. So, it was a fun interview. I hope you enjoyed it. Well, honored to be here with you, Alex, at GTC. This is our first podcast I've recording anywhere except my basement or the office. So, this is pretty exciting. I have to start by asking you to tell me the story of Wave because I think it's an especially interesting one. Yeah, actually it's a fun I just I just got give from giving a speech here at the event and I put the put my face up on the big screen in front of a 50,000 person stadium before like 5 minutes before Jensen came on and it reminded me because the last I've only spoken in front of like 50,000 people twice in my life. And the last time was at a conference called Web Web Summit back in 2018 where we had this you know seed company pitch competition where I got to the the final and had to give a I think it was like a 3-minute pitch in front of the crowd. And long story short, there was a crowd vote that I ended up coming last in. Yet, the judges gave the competition to me because they I don't know they they they they liked what I said, but just gives you the idea of 10 years ago when I started the company, what we were doing was completely contrarian. The idea that So, what Wave started with a thesis that autonomous driving is all about looking at the the AV problem with an AI approach. So, it's a decision-making complex reasoning challenge. And we took the approach of the best way to go tackle that is with end-to-end learning. And a decade ago, we started working on this building a single model that could reason and strive and generalize in the largest scale possible without the AV 1.0, the first generation approach which relied on retrofit sensors, compute, HD maps, infrastructure, and really limited scaling. And what's And so, we started that a decade ago when this was really laughed at and dismissed by the industry. And we've sort of quietly building it away. And we're at the point now where not only is the market industry excited about AVs again, but we have an AI model that we're now the first company to have driven zero shot in over 500 cities. So, this means this model is driven throughout Europe, Asia, North America. It's also driven in over 10 different cars from from electric vehicles to vans to SUVs. And so, it's now emerging as an AI model capable of driving any vehicle anywhere. And we've got a a sprint ahead of us over the next year to get this deployed in both robo-taxis and consumer vehicles you can buy. You know, I remember having a really amazing experience driving with you in London where you let me decide where I wanted the model to go. And I hadn't spent much time in London, and I was astonished by how challenging it seemed compared to San Francisco where people I think obey the traffic laws as I understand it much more thoroughly than in than in London. And there's just much more surprising things coming at you. And I couldn't believe how well your model works at that time. And I think this was maybe two or three But but before we get to the the quality of your model, I I kind of want to go back in time a little bit and ask you about your your history, right? You grew up in a farm, I think, in New Zealand. Is that right? Yeah, in Christchurch in the South Island. And how do you think that's kind of affected your leadership or your thinking about autonomous vehicles? Um, I think there are a few things that were really important to me growing up on reflection. I mean, I spent half my life um, building things, whatever it was video games or different Lego or robotics or other contraptions. I spent a lot of time messing around with that kind of stuff. And the other half was in the mountains climbing climbing mountains, surfing, mountain biking, all this kind of stuff. And I called Wave Wave because I want riding a car to feel as good as surfing a wave. But I really think, you know, when it comes to pushing forward frontier technology, it's all about an adventure. It's all about doing something where you have to plan, you have to risk manage, you have to be ambitious, push boundaries. And I think there's a bit of a bit of that that I I got from those experiences and those values. Uh, um, that that I that we can take forward today because what we've been doing, it's it's one of those ones where when we started, this this wasn't possible, but I thought and hoped and it's turned out that the future building this technology would become possible. And I think we've been lucky that so many things have come together that that make that a reality, but it it really did seem like optimizing for the I was fortunate to get a scholarship to take me from New Zealand to Cambridge University. Turning up to that place was like like a castle from the Middle Ages full of the most amazing brilliant minds to just learn and explore. Again, like optimizing for adventures is somewhat led me to where we are today. Okay, you got to tell me about building the first end-to-end prototype because I would imagine that would be particularly hard with the way that you're approaching the problem. You can't really cheat, right? If you're trying to do end-to-end machine learning. Tell me about the experience of that and how you made it work. So, in 2017, I just finished my PhD thesis and I'd be able to build the first deep learning model for different perception tasks, localization, stereo vision, semantic segmentation, uncertainty estimation, all of these kind of systems that could take high-dimensional, multi-million dimensional images and turn them into compressed representations of the world learning end-to-end. At the same time, friends down the road at at DeepMind had just produced AlphaGo. That's amazing. That's a system that's like there's more states of the game of Go than there are atoms in the universe. Extraordinarily complex. And they showed through self-play you could learn in simulation you could learn an agent that could beat the world champion. And so, I saw these things and I thought, "Okay, we can now understand the complexity of the real world through computer vision. If you have unlimited data through a simulator in a very low-dimensional state, you can solve the hardest game there is of Go. Now it's time to bring these together into the physical world, which I've always been excited about about physical mobility and adventure and robotics. Um, and so I thought we could go try it. So, what we did is we raised 1 and 1/2 million dollars, got some friends together, we rented a house, and we put our car in the got a car in the garage and started hacking away. And the first thing we tried was on-policy reinforcement learning where we put the car on a road and say, "Okay, go drive with the reward of drive as far as you can without any human intervention." And your reward is distance traveled. And so, the car starts driving randomly. And essentially, over period of months, we develop an algorithm that Q-learning algorithm that essentially lets you intervene and and grab the wheel every time it tries to drive off the road. And the big breakthrough was the moment where with just 10 bits of intervention, we got it to learn how to lane follow. And I got that breakthrough I remember it was on like a Sunday or a Saturday it was on a weekend where I was like pushing really hard, nothing was working. So, these kind of algorithms tended to work one out of 10 times. >> Wait, let me make sure I understand. So, you only intervened, it had no prior information about the world. It's just looking at a camera. Yeah. And after 10 interventions, it understands that you wanted to follow lanes and it can follow lanes? That's right. So, the input was a front camera. There was a GPU on board doing on-policy optimization sorry, onboard optimization. And it was essentially, yeah, trial and error, a few different 10 trials, and then it was able to learn a policy. There's a video on YouTube that's it's got this that we muted the audio cuz the audio is me whooping and cheering as it starts to drive, but it was essentially just able to lane follow quite robustly after just 10 bits of feedback with no prior knowledge of the world. Were you afraid when you got in the car the first time and it had no prior information? Like were you actually on a road? It was going slowly, so you had time to intervene before it drives off the road. So, I but but that brings up a good question is that that method obviously doesn't scale. You can't do on-policy tests at at scale around the world. That's that's pretty unsafe. And so, we quickly moved to a imitation and offline reinforcement learning paradigm and started scaling up world models so we could learn through dreaming and and understanding through their experience. And so, later in 2018, that's where we started to take things. And growth there has brought us to this point today where we can drive any vehicle anywhere all around the world. So, what happened after you got the lane following? What was kind of the next step? Next step was starting to scale it up. So, we tried so many different things. We tried injecting priors around computer vision. We tried building world models and doing learning in in dreaming and learning in in in imagination states. We tried imitation learning. We essentially and we tried sim-to-real learning in simulation transfer to the real world. We just sort of tried everything. We wrote all these blog posts that ended up being industry first in all those different areas. Uh and then settled on what a recipe that seemed to work, seemed to scale, and then started really pushing, had a couple of years of pushing infrastructure, fleet buildout, and and actually bringing up this technology to a point where it could start to drive in London. And retrospectively, learning in London was great because it forced us to build something that could deal with the you know, the chaos and complexity of your average London street. You have like a hundred pedestrians and cyclists around you at any one time. Uh and and so it forced us to build something that didn't rely on mapping infrastructure or anything like that, but it could actually um you know, drive in the city that's got it's 2,000 years old. I remember 10 years ago when you were getting started, you know, time in this vehicle space was was super crowded with lots of different approaches, lots of VC money flowing in. It seems like it's consolidated at this point, but also it seems like it's really working. I use autopilot in my Tesla all the time. I read Waymos every day. I feel like it's kind of quietly snuck up and become a technology that at least here in San Francisco we use all the time. I'm curious how you think about the competition at this point. Is it becoming a commodity? Is there something different about what Wave can do or how you approach this? Yeah, that's a good question because I think broadly speaking when we started in 2017, that was probably the peak of the hype cycle where raising 1 and 1/2 million dollars to go tackle what was it like 10 companies that just announced billion-dollar ambitions and deals um was a bit lopsided, but it's fair to say we saw a you know, trough of disillusionment um and I think largely that was from the sheer expense, the hundred billion-plus dollars that's required to build that AV 1.0 paradigm of mapping, rules, infrastructure, and to get that to maturity. And there was a lot of consolidation there where really only, you know, one or two companies had the uh appetite of capital to push through and make that make that work. At the same time, Tesla FSD or ourselves matured an AV 2.0 approach, a next-generation stack that's more affordable, scalable, and has now gone to the point from a a demo which we had 5 years ago to a point where it's actually ready for global automotive product. And I think the most exciting thing today is we're also seeing huge validation of the product. You and many others are now buying uh FSD and paying for it and it's it's it's it's actually loved by a lot of customers. When you go to Shanghai and San Francisco, places like this, and people will pay, even pay more than a a human taxi experience for a robotaxi, even though it has longer wait times, um it's limited by a geofence, uh and and it's it's a product that the people really love. And so now the question is, how do you bring this to any vehicle anywhere? And I think that's the step change that that we've built and we can offer. Taking it from expensive retrofit vehicles to mass-market vehicles that uh you can buy or manufacture for, you know, 30, 40, 50,000 dollars each that have inbuilt uh hardware that's in global supply chains that doesn't need an HD map, so can drive anywhere, uh and it's got the flexibility to be in a fleet that you ride-hail or in a car that you own. Um and so I think and by the way, this this this ambition, this business model is only available because we've built this generalizable AI driver. Um but I think now the question is, how do you bring it to global scale? And that's the the shift that we want to take the industry through. Um but to me, I'm I'm I'm thrilled that we're now seeing uh what what are the conditions in the market? So I mentioned that um people are now, you know, paying and there's real commercial validation of this as a product. Regulation is in place, not just in the US, but we work really closely with the UK, Europe, and Japan. We co-chair the UN uh committee on on uh D cast regulation for autonomous systems uh that later this year is is now going to come through and actually legalize these products. Um and then of course, manufacturers are now building vehicles at millions of scale volume that have centralized compute, surround sensors, and the ability to over-the-air update and get data off them, which makes a fleet learning product possible. So all of these factors together give me a lot of optimism for going through a a massive commercial inflection point in the coming year. It's kind of interesting you didn't mention in that list improving the algorithm. And it's very hard to judge the quality of an algorithm or the safety of a self-driving car when you're in it. But I have to say when I was inside of your car, and this is now I think 2 years ago, it really felt ready for primetime. I think it felt safer than the Cruise cars that were on the street in San Francisco, which I don't know if I should trust my own judgment or not. But are there still things to do with the algorithm to get it to the point where you feel good about using it globally or is that no longer the challenge and you've kind of moved on to logistics and regulation and things like that? Absolutely, there are still things to do. We don't yet have a driverless service today, but this is no longer the critical path. We are seeing that the quality of innovation coming from our frontier embodied AI science team, plus the data and compute growth that we have, is just driving compounding uh uh scale. And then you layer on top of uh deeper integration into vehicles with our partners, you get a um uh sensing and compute on the vehicles. These things compound that performance is no longer the critical path, but we still have work to do there. Um uh and so we'll keep keep pushing that and and pushing it into a generalized state. Um uh I think a large challenge is evaluation and validation of it. How do you prove the level of performance is sufficient to yourselves, to regulators, to consumers? Uh and then of course, commercially deploying and setting up, I mean uh the last couple of years for us has been a big year of growth of the company to build um strength of team, to partner with regulators and automotive and fleets in major markets like Germany, Japan, and the US. It's to get the integration going. It's the multi-year sales cycle of automotive, and now it's going to be the deployment and bring up of of these systems. And so I think that's where the the critical path is, but we're not taking the foot off the accelerator in terms of performance, which um you know, clearly clearly has orders of magnitude still still to go uh in terms of uh uh opportunity ahead. One of my favorite demos I've ever seen was someone on your team who showed me plugging your perception model into an LLM so that you could actually see what the car was thinking as it was driving, which seemed surprising and delightful. Was that a toy thing that you realized you didn't need or is that something that you've continued to invest in? Yeah, you know what? In 2021 or 22, like way before ChatGPT, one of my um uh team, Vijay, came to me and said, "Hey Alex, I want to start working on language for driving." And my first reaction was, "No way, man. This is we're going to stay focused. We're never product shipped. Come on, uh stay focused on on on driving." And he he laid out the argument that actually um the knowledge you can get from text data is enormous and uh it might produce new interesting products and interaction modalities. Uh and and and actually by bringing in language, uh we can improve the representation, the performance, and and and the product experience. And so um we actually said, "Okay, let's let's start exploring this." Uh and so very quickly we got up a prototype that it actually wasn't plugging together an LLM because LLMs didn't really exist in that large scale yet. It was more um which trained a vision-language-action model. We trained a single model that could see the world, drive a car, and understand language. Uh and so it started off as a gimmick, you know, you could drive along and have it explain its driving, you know, "I'm going around a double-parked bus. I'm stopping for a red light." And we'd just explain it. And you know, you could cherry-pick some really delightful things, but then again, there were also some things where it was just hallucinating and wrong. Um but from that point, we started to uh you know, train it up. Uh we actually got it to a point where it would become very robust. Um we saw that there was there was actually one interesting thing I saw, which is if you drive and you have an oncoming car and you want to make a turn across that oncoming car to to go into a side street, if that oncoming car stops and flashes its lights at you, it's a signal for you to go in front, right? Even though you don't have right of way. Our AI wouldn't do that. It would stop and yield and just wait for that car cuz it had right of way. When we started bringing language pre-training into uh into the VLA model, it actually demonstrated that behavior. When it started flashing its lights, it would now now it would now turn. I saw that for the first time. And and so that was an example of we could actually improve the representation and reasoning capabilities by bringing in language into um the system. And so today, it's uh core part of our training. Uh it gives us boost in performance. It's opening up new interpretability capabilities. Um and of course, opens up product experiences of personalization and interaction uh with the vehicle. Um you can now get in our car and prompt it to drive in different driving styles, and I think that'll be a neat experience cuz when you get into a ADAS or a robotaxi driver assistance for a robotaxi car, if that car is driving too conservatively, you're going to get really frustrated. Or if it's driving too fast for you, you're going to get freaked out. And different people have quite wide variety and different expectations there. Or different brands. You can go out some brands that want to be sporty or some brands that want to be safe and uh and reliable, and um not say the sporty won't be safe and reliable, but you know what I mean. Uh and so providing that personalization uh is quite a nice outcome of of this work. It's a funny moment for itself-driving at least in San Francisco where we suddenly have Waymos everywhere. And I I love it. I think it's an amazing experience, but we're also witnessing all kinds of different crazy traffic issues that are being caused. And also, people, maybe for fun or maybe maliciously, kind of messing with them in ways that that you wouldn't have expected. So it does seem like that capability might actually be completely critical to making a self-driving car a system people trust. Yes, although a couple of things. Firstly, our cars uh you know, over time you won't notice anything different cuz they're just normal cars with built-in uh sensors. So, I hope that people don't behave differently around them compared to other other traffic. Um and then secondly, our system is designed to drive in a very human-like way. The way you see it um picking up and dropping off as a robotaxi experience in busy cities or the way you see it nudging through crowds of pedestrians and doing things that are very human-like and not just being stuck. Uh I I think these kind of things are really going to improve public acceptance. We design the system to operate in the messy cities we live in and not require infrastructural behavior change. I think that's really important for adoption of embodied AI. You need to use mass-market hardware. You need to be able to leverage availability of off-policy data uh and you need to be able to operate as a drop-in to existing infrastructure, not requiring massive cap exchanges. I think if you get these things right, uh then embodied AI is a delightful deployment experience. How did you make the decision to not build your own car and to license your technology to other car companies? That that was very clear to us from the beginning. I I I think the mindset I've always and we've always had as a company is that self-driving is an AI problem. We want to work on the hardest problem first. So, we started working on AI and we started aggressively partnering on cloud, on car, on infrastructure, on insurance and and working with the best companies around us um so that we can focus on the critical path problem. And by always focusing on the hardest problem first, it's as kind of you know, not going to stumble into some glass ceiling that we hit down down down the road. So, I think that's that it was that mindset. But then retrospectively, it's become important because I think it's a mistake to focus on one vehicle form factor because it's really unclear what will have product market fit. Is it a normal SUV like today? Is it a bidirectional vehicle where you sit facing each other? Is it a two-seater electric vehicle? Uh uh or are there other applications that really take off? Um and I think that's unclear, but at the end of the day, um if you focus on one form factor, you're also going to get have a a small part of the market. And so, we've tried to focus on how do we build a generalizable driver that can address everything and then follow where the market is um most, you know, advanced at any one stage. We started off with retrofit when there wasn't really anything for us to integrate into. Then during COVID, grocery and fleet started uh really skyrocketing in profits, and so we started partnering with that sector. Then things changed. Uh but then automotive, who would never speak to us that said this is unsafe, it would never work, all of a sudden started talking to us and building software-defined vehicles so we can integrate with them. And now today, we've got the excitement of robotaxis. So, we've kind of followed the market, but been open to integrate into anything. Um and the end state, of course, is uh consumer vehicles, delivery, robotaxi, trucking, non-automotive forms of robotics. We want to license our embodied AI and make all of them intelligent, safe, and uh and and possible. Where you sit right now as CEO, do you have like a stack ranking of those different modalities and which one's most top of mind for you? Yeah, today it's all about consumer vehicles and robotaxis. It's about point-to-point driving in urban and highway situations with uh mass-market uh consumer vehicle hardware. So, this is an aligned product that we can um now commercialize with great partners. So, this is this is the real focus for us today that can generate you know, really extraordinary business. Um I mean, you look at the scale of uh uh you know, whether it's data, revenue, number of vehicles, exposure. About 100 million cars produced each year. Um and people forget that. Often, they only think of robotaxis, but there's less than 10,000 robotaxis in the world. And so, getting the getting this natively integrated into consumer vehicles opens up tens of millions of vehicle opportunity uh that's going to be far outscale robotaxis in the short and medium term. Before long term, of course, every vehicle is capable of of driverless operation, which is clearly the steady state of where we're going to go to uh given the safety and convenience and um product benefits that it can bring. What geographies do you plan to launch in first? I mean, we're we're really looking to make sure the system is global, but we're focused today on Europe, including the UK, as well as Japan and North America. Uh so, we have um fleets and offices in uh London, Stuttgart, Tokyo, and the Bay Area. We've driven around most cities across throughout Europe and uh North America uh and and growing in in in Japan. And those are the the markets we're starting with uh given the the customers we work with. Do you have a sense of where you will go mass market first? It's it's it's those markets. I think in general, um we're seeing faster movement in North America and Japan, closely followed by Europe. Um but of course, uh China is uh unfortunately, it's very hard for us to work in China and vice versa. Um uh I won't get into geopolitics, but it's a it's a bit of a shame that's where the the world's at. But in China, uh this technology is moving so quickly and the rate of innovation is very impressive. Um and so, I think it's it's worth looking at if uh if you haven't from an automotive technology perspective. Uh another proof point that when this technology is deployed, consumers really love it. And it's now a alongside um price and safety, it's now a top three reason why people buy a car in China now. Wow. There's autonomy. So, you had amazing results at the AI 500 road show last year. I don't know if you want to talk about it, but you took one single AI model deployed all over the place with I think many of those geographies actually not having any prior training data on it. Were you were surprised that it worked so well? Uh it's no, because that was the core thesis that by building a general-purpose AI model, we could drive anywhere. But to actually see it was uh was huge validation. Um so yeah, we drove around the world. Uh some some very cool anecdotes. I mean, we drove in places like around the Arc de Triomphe in Paris. Have you have you driven around there? I have, yeah. Well, not driven, but I I've gone there, yeah. It's chaos, right? Uh but yeah, we went north of the Arctic Circle in Finland and Sweden um and tested driving in 22-hour darkness, and snow, and things like that. Um we're even in Tokyo during a typhoon, where local trains and buses shut down. And actually, that's how we were giving a bunch of media drives. We had a day long of journalist drives, no disengagements, perfect autonomy the whole day, in literally the heaviest rain I've ever seen. Um and our cameras are situated just behind the windshield, the front camera. Windshield wiper was just constantly wiping water off it. Uh but it drove reliably around central Tokyo, which was um quite remarkable. Less pedestrians on the road that day, but still busy busy traffic. But um yeah, we've we've seen all of these things uh in that, and it demonstrated as the first company to do something of that scale. And it demonstrated that we truly can generalize to any vehicle anywhere um given that over half of these uh cities we had no prior training data in. And so, it was true zero-shot generalization test at scale. Mhm. That almost sounds like superhuman-level performance. Yeah, I'd be I'd be careful with that word. I mean, you and I, if we jump in a new city, you can um get a rental car and go drive there. But uh to claim superhuman, I mean, there's some great reports that I read one from Swiss Re, for example, with the insurer, where they measured uh uh the impact of a uh autonomous system of of Waymo operating uh of of their operational history and found that they had uh much fewer accidents and and safety events than the equivalent uh human drivers. So, I think there's some robust evidence that's really showing autonomy can be superhuman. Um uh we have work to do to demonstrate that, uh but we're making good progress there. But then you also look at um uh sadly, over 99% of accidents are caused due to human error. And so, even just making a a self-driving experience that can never be distracted, drunk, impaired, that can see 360° all at once, make decisions over 10 times a second, uh I mean, there is an opportunity there to drive accidents down to near zero. Um and that's what we want to see, but I think um you're going to see scale deployment to really show that. And uh we want to go do that in the coming years. Are there situations that still make you nervous at this moment? What what would be the kind of configuration that would put you on the edge of what the model can do? I think it's very hard to give general statements to that because where the models today tend to struggle is when you have a culmination of many failure modes. If it's you know, dark, you have high-speed traffic, you have adversarial behavior from someone else driving on the wrong side of the road or not yielding. Um maybe it's it's bad weather. You know, it's when you have multiple confounding things. Then maybe have a sensor failure. When you get multiple confounding factors, that's when you tend to find uh challenges and failure modes. And so, um we need to keep addressing that with uh better uh system resiliency or redundancy. We're we're building that with some next-generation vehicle architectures. Uh we need more data to improve the generalization and risk assessment. Uh and um yeah, making sure we deploy responsibly in areas that uh we're confident meet uh you know, meet the required bar for safety. Um but we'll do that responsibly with our partners as as as we grow. And I think the other nice thing about being deployed in consumer vehicles and robotaxis, starting with supervised trials, is that we can incrementally grow this with communities and regulators to the end state where every vehicle is autonomous at a global scale. I remember there was a moment in sort of ethics in AI, maybe when AI was like when self-driving was hot, that people were asking questions, maybe toy questions about should a car prioritize the safety of the passenger versus a pedestrian. And you know, and end to end, you're kind of sidestepping those questions and maybe you're not inserting yourself. Is that true, or do you need to Yeah. push the data in certain ways to to get certain types of ethical behavior that you want? So, this is the trolley problem. We have to We've got two bad decisions, and you have to choose bad decision A or bad decision B. There's many ways of framing it. The practical reality is as an industry, and I think I speak on behalf of most AV companies for this, is that we need to design a system so there's always a third good option. It's called a minimal risk maneuver, which is if you're in a bad scenario, you try to um and you you you see risk, there's always a third option you can take, which is to minimize risk. And that typically involves reducing kinetic energy, maintaining a predictable course of action, uh and stopping or pulling over the vehicle or something like this. And we always need to prepare to do that if any systems fail, there needs to be a fail operational ability to do that. And so we design these systems to always have a third good choice, not to be stuck between two bad choices. The second point is that to get into that two bad choice state, so many things have to go wrong to get to that point. And the probability of those kind of scenarios is so small. And even if you are in that position, the probability that you can actually accurately sense in those scenarios that those two things, it makes this more of a theoretical thought exercise than actual practical decision the industry needs to take. Um uh so we focus on and the industry focuses on building these minimal risk maneuvers that take us away from those situations. Having said that, if you do get into that unlikely uh situation where you're in there, then of course uh this the the behavior will depend on how you bias it from your training data and the system that you design. Um and so there, of course, we need to be thoughtful with the principles um uh and the knowledge that we we distill into the system and the behavior that that that we generate. I mean, there are some some good principles there around uh managing risk, um uh making the system predictable, and of course, maximizing safety.
